title: Monoculture vs. Polyculture Ecosystem Comparison
creator: Dan McCreary
subject: Ecology
description: Split-screen p5.js simulation comparing pest spread, drought resilience, and yield stability between monoculture and polyculture farming systems

learningObjectives: Compare the ecological resilience of monoculture and polyculture systems under stress
Analyze how crop diversity affects long-term soil health and yield stability
Evaluate the trade-offs between monoculture efficiency and polyculture resilience
prerequisites: Familiarity with biodiversity and ecosystem services
Introduction to soil science and nutrient cycling

pedagogical.teachingStrategy: Side-by-side comparison with controlled stress experiments
keyQuestions: Why do pests spread faster when all plants in a field are the same species?
How does plant diversity help a field retain soil moisture during drought?
What happens to soil health after many seasons of monoculture versus polyculture?
Why might a farmer choose monoculture despite its ecological disadvantages?
How do the simulation results connect to real-world agricultural sustainability?
commonMisconceptions: Monoculture always produces more food -- while per-season yields may be higher initially, cumulative yield often favors polyculture due to soil degradation
Polyculture means lower productivity -- diverse systems can match or exceed monoculture yields when measured over multiple seasons
Pest resistance is mainly about pesticides -- crop diversity itself is a powerful pest management strategy
assessmentOpportunities: Have students graph cumulative yield over 20 seasons and identify the crossover point
Ask students to design a polyculture planting plan for a specific climate and soil type
Compare simulation data to published research on monoculture vs. polyculture yields
